nodes.context: Mexican drug cartel and organized crime syndicate, formerly headed by the five Beltrán Leyva brothers.
Mexican drug cartel, originally the parent organization of the Beltrán-Leyva Organization.
Mexican drug cartel, rival of the Beltrán-Leyva Organization.
Mexican criminal organization, allied with the Beltrán-Leyva Organization.
Gang formed by former Beltrán-Leyva lieutenant Óscar Osvaldo García Montoya to regroup cartel remnants.
Mexican drug cartel, allied with the Beltrán-Leyva Organization after the split from Sinaloa Cartel.
Mexican drug cartel, allied with Gulf Cartel and Sinaloa Cartel against Beltrán-Leyva and Los Zetas.
Colombian drug cartel, former supplier of cocaine to the Beltrán-Leyva Organization.
Armed loyalist wing of the Beltrán-Leyva brothers, currently fighting the Sinaloa Cartel.
Former Beltrán-Leyva enforcement squad, loyal to Edgar Valdez Villarreal after infighting.
Elite hitsquad loyal to Arturo Beltrán Leyva.
Splinter group of the Beltrán-Leyva Organization in Guerrero, currently fighting La Barredora.
Splinter group in Aguascalientes and Zacatecas, believed to be run by Chapo Isidro.
Hitsquad with presence in the US.
Group in Guerrero, currently fighting the Independent Cartel of Acapulco.
Short-lived cell in Morelos created by Héctor Beltrán Leyva.
Former enforcers of the Beltrán-Leyva brothers, now part of the Gulf Cartel.
Former enforcers of the Beltrán-Leyva brothers, apparently independent in Guerrero.
Splinter group in Morelos and Guerrero.
Cell of Guerreros Unidos in Tierra Caliente that specializes in extorting politicians.
Mexican drug cartel, listed as ally in infobox.
Prison gang and criminal organization, listed as ally in infobox.
Mexican drug cartel, listed as ally in infobox.
Mexican criminal group, listed as ally in infobox.
Transnational criminal gang, listed as rival in infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: Founded as a Sinaloa Cartel, the Beltrán Leyva cartel was responsible for transportation and wholesaling of cocaine, heroin and marijuana (and the production of the last two).
edges.context: The Beltrán-Leyva Organization was originally part of the Sinaloa Cartel.

edges.evidence_quote: The renewed fighting in Sinaloa state between the BLO and the Cartel is supposedly sparked by the incursion of the Cartel and its allies in Nuevo Laredo, traditionally the biggest Zeta stronghold.
edges.context: The Beltrán-Leyva Organization and the Sinaloa Cartel are in conflict, fighting each other in Sinaloa.

edges.evidence_quote: Sensing a void in the rival Gulf Cartel after Osiel Cárdenas' arrest on March 14, 2003, the organization began to move into Gulf Cartel territory. The gangs fought each other in northern Mexican cities, resulting in the deaths of hundreds of people, including some civilians, police and journalists.
edges.context: The Beltrán-Leyva Organization fought the Gulf Cartel over territory.

edges.evidence_quote: Allied forces of Los Zetas and Beltrán-Leyva remnants clashed on April 28, 2012 with gunmen of the organization in the Choix mountains.
edges.context: Los Zetas and Beltrán-Leyva remnants were allied and fought together against another organization.

edges.evidence_quote: The Beltrán Leyva brothers, and those loyalists who departed the Cartel with them, allied with Los Zetas, causing an escalation of conflict in strongholds shared uneasily by 'old' leaders.
edges.context: The Beltrán-Leyva Organization allied with Los Zetas after splitting from the Sinaloa Cartel.

edges.evidence_quote: In February 2010, the Beltrán Leyva Cartel and Los Zetas engaged in a violent turf war against the new alliance integrated by the Gulf Cartel, Sinaloa Cartel and La Familia Cartel in the border city of Reynosa, Tamaulipas, rendering some border towns 'ghost towns'.
edges.context: The Beltrán-Leyva Organization and Los Zetas fought together against the Gulf Cartel, Sinaloa Cartel, and La Familia Cartel.

edges.evidence_quote: The residual impact of Alfredo's arrest not only undermined long-term alliances, but resurrected animosities between rival cartel leaders Joaquin 'El Chapo' Guzmán and Arturo's new allies, the Juárez Cartel, and provided the catalyst behind the bloodshed in Mexico's most violent city: Ciudad Juárez.
edges.context: The Beltrán-Leyva Organization allied with the Juárez Cartel after splitting from the Sinaloa Cartel.

edges.evidence_quote: In February 2010, the Beltrán Leyva Cartel and Los Zetas engaged in a violent turf war against the new alliance integrated by the Gulf Cartel, Sinaloa Cartel and La Familia Cartel in the border city of Reynosa, Tamaulipas, rendering some border towns 'ghost towns'.
edges.context: The Beltrán-Leyva Organization fought against La Familia Cartel as part of a larger conflict.

edges.evidence_quote: After that, the organization pursued a relationship with Víctor and Darío Espinoza Valencia of Colombia's Norte del Valle cartel.
edges.context: The Beltrán-Leyva Organization sought a supplier relationship with the Norte del Valle Cartel.

edges.evidence_quote: Los Mazatlecos in Sinaloa and Nayarit, an armed loyalist wing of the Beltrán Leyva brothers, currently fighting the Sinaloa Cartel in northern Sinaloa.
edges.context: Los Mazatlecos is a loyalist wing of the Beltrán-Leyva Organization.

edges.evidence_quote: Los Negros a former Beltran Leyva enforcement squad, loyal to Edgar Valdez Villarreal after infighting broke out
edges.context: Los Negros was originally an enforcement squad of the Beltrán-Leyva Organization.

edges.evidence_quote: Fuerzas Especiales De Arturo (Special Forces of Arturo), an elite hitsquad loyal to Arturo Beltran Leyva
edges.context: Fuerzas Especiales De Arturo is an elite hitsquad loyal to Arturo Beltrán Leyva, part of the organization.

edges.evidence_quote: Independent Cartel of Acapulco in Guerrero, currently fighting La Barredora.
edges.context: The Independent Cartel of Acapulco is a splinter group of the Beltrán-Leyva Organization.

edges.evidence_quote: La Oficina In Aguascalientes and Zacatecas. Believed to be run by Chapo Isidro current leader of the Beltran Leyva organization along with the Velasco family well known in the state of Aguascalientes
edges.context: La Oficina is a splinter group of the Beltrán-Leyva Organization.

edges.evidence_quote: Los Charritos Most active hitsquad believed to have a heavy presence in the US (Chicago, Atlanta, Los Angeles, Las Vegas)
edges.context: Los Charritos is a hitsquad that splintered from the Beltrán-Leyva Organization.

edges.evidence_quote: La Barredora in Guerrero, currently fighting the Independent Cartel of Acapulco
edges.context: La Barredora is a splinter group of the Beltrán-Leyva Organization.

edges.evidence_quote: In April 2010, Héctor Beltrán Leyva created a short-lived cell or branch in Morelos state called South Pacific Cartel (Cártel del Pacífico Sur) best known for having employed a 12-year-old gunman and executioner.
edges.context: The South Pacific Cartel was a short-lived cell created by Héctor Beltrán Leyva as part of the organization.

edges.evidence_quote: Los Pelones in Morelos. These are former enforcers of the Beltrán-Leyva brothers, now part of the Gulf Cartel.
edges.context: Los Pelones were former enforcers of the Beltrán-Leyva Organization.

edges.evidence_quote: Los Rojos Cartel, in Guerrero. These are former enforcers of the Beltrán-Leyva brothers, apparently independent and dominating much of Guerrero.
edges.context: Los Rojos Cartel were former enforcers of the Beltrán-Leyva Organization.

edges.evidence_quote: Guerreros Unidos (United Warriors) in Morelos and Guerrero
edges.context: Guerreros Unidos is a splinter group of the Beltrán-Leyva Organization.

edges.evidence_quote: Los Tequileros, a cell of Guerreros Unidos in Tierra Caliente that specializes in extorting politicians
edges.context: Los Tequileros is a cell of Guerreros Unidos, which is a splinter of the Beltrán-Leyva Organization.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Los Mazatlecos, Los Zetas, Juarez Cartel, Independent Cartel of Acapulco, Knights Templar Cartel, Barrio Azteca, Tijuana Cartel, La Línea
edges.context: The Knights Templar Cartel is listed as an ally in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Los Mazatlecos, Los Zetas, Juarez Cartel, Independent Cartel of Acapulco, Knights Templar Cartel, Barrio Azteca, Tijuana Cartel, La Línea
edges.context: Barrio Azteca is listed as an ally in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Los Mazatlecos, Los Zetas, Juarez Cartel, Independent Cartel of Acapulco, Knights Templar Cartel, Barrio Azteca, Tijuana Cartel, La Línea
edges.context: The Tijuana Cartel is listed as an ally in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Los Mazatlecos, Los Zetas, Juarez Cartel, Independent Cartel of Acapulco, Knights Templar Cartel, Barrio Azteca, Tijuana Cartel, La Línea
edges.context: La Línea is listed as an ally in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: La Familia Michoacana, Gulf Cartel, MS-13, Sinaloa Cartel
edges.context: La Familia Michoacana is listed as a rival in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: La Familia Michoacana, Gulf Cartel, MS-13, Sinaloa Cartel
edges.context: The Gulf Cartel is listed as a rival in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: La Familia Michoacana, Gulf Cartel, MS-13, Sinaloa Cartel
edges.context: MS-13 is listed as a rival in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: La Familia Michoacana, Gulf Cartel, MS-13, Sinaloa Cartel
edges.context: The Sinaloa Cartel is listed as a rival in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: Independent Cartel of Acapulco in Guerrero, currently fighting La Barredora.
edges.context: The Independent Cartel of Acapulco and La Barredora are currently fighting each other.

edges.evidence_quote: Los Mazatlecos in Sinaloa and Nayarit, an armed loyalist wing of the Beltrán Leyva brothers, currently fighting the Sinaloa Cartel in northern Sinaloa.
edges.context: Los Mazatlecos are currently fighting the Sinaloa Cartel.

edges.evidence_quote: Los Pelones in Morelos. These are former enforcers of the Beltrán-Leyva brothers, now part of the Gulf Cartel.
edges.context: Los Pelones are now part of the Gulf Cartel.

edges.context: Los Tequileros is a cell of Guerreros Unidos.
